construct_description: The ability to evaluate competing operational and patient safety perspectives, engage constructively with team members, and drive toward a sound process improvement recommendation — a core competency for pharmacy technicians and supervisors involved in workflow design and medication safety.

collaboration_construct.name: Collaboration Quality
collaboration_construct.description: How effectively the participant engages with teammates — building on ideas, challenging constructively, and synthesizing toward consensus.

dimensions.name: Engagement
dimensions.description: Actively participates and responds to teammates

dimensions.name: Building
dimensions.description: References, quotes, and extends others' ideas

dimensions.name: Challenging
dimensions.description: Pushes back constructively with safety reasoning

dimensions.name: Synthesizing
dimensions.description: Combines viewpoints and moves toward a decision

domain_dimensions.name: Safety Reasoning
domain_dimensions.description: Applies medication safety principles to the workflow decision

domain_dimensions.name: Operational Fit
domain_dimensions.description: Evaluates whether the proposed change is feasible given staffing and volume

domain_dimensions.name: Evidence Use
domain_dimensions.description: References error rate data, benchmarks, or safety research in arguments

domain_dimensions.name: Decision Quality
domain_dimensions.description: Final recommendation is well-justified, patient-centered, and implementable

scenario.title: The Verification Workflow Question
scenario.prompt: Your pharmacy quality team must decide whether to implement a new two-pharmacist final verification requirement for all high-alert medications before dispensing. The change was proposed after a near-miss error at a sister location. You need a recommendation for the pharmacy director by end of day.

teammates.name: Patel
teammates.title: Staff Pharmacist
teammates.trait: Patient safety first — supports the change strongly, but acknowledges workflow realities. Tends to underweight operational burden on the team.

teammates.name: Marcus
teammates.title: Lead Pharmacy Technician
teammates.trait: On the floor every day — deeply skeptical about adding steps to an already stretched workflow. Has seen change proposals fail before because they ignored volume realities.

teammates.name: Lin
teammates.title: Pharmacy Quality Coordinator
teammates.trait: Data-driven and pragmatic — wants the right answer but needs evidence and a realistic implementation path before committing.

decision_options.name: Full Implementation — All High-Alert Meds, All Shifts
decision_options.desc: Two-pharmacist verification required for all ISMP high-alert medications at all locations, all shifts. No exceptions.

decision_options.name: Limited Implementation — High-Volume High-Alert Meds Only
decision_options.desc: Two-pharmacist verification for the top-5 highest-error-rate high-alert medications only (insulin, heparin, concentrated electrolytes, opioids, chemotherapy).

decision_options.name: Tech-Check-Tech Program for Routine Fills
decision_options.desc: Implement a certified Tech-Check-Tech (TCT) program for routine fills, freeing pharmacist time for high-alert final verification without adding total verification burden.

decision_options.name: Defer — Pilot at One Location First
decision_options.desc: Run a 60-day pilot at the highest-volume location, measure wait time and error rate impact, then decide on system-wide implementation.

scenario.reference_data_html:
High-Alert Medication Error Data — Meridian Pharmacy Group (Last 12 Months)

Medication ClassError EventsNear-Misses% of Total Errors
Insulin (all types)41134%
Anticoagulants (heparin, warfarin)3724%
Concentrated Electrolytes1411%
Opioids (high-dose, pediatric)2311%
Oral Chemotherapy025%
All other high-alert meds139%

The near-miss that prompted this discussion: insulin 10 units/mL vial dispensed when 100 units/mL was prescribed — caught by nurse at bedside, not at pharmacy. Near-miss, not a harm event.

Benchmarks

  • ISMP recommends two-pharmacist independent double-check for high-alert medications — endorsed but 'not always operationally feasible'
  • Hospitals with double-check programs: 73% report reduction in high-alert medication errors
  • Average wait time increase with two-pharmacist verification: +4.2 minutes per prescription (ASHP study, 2023)
  • Tech-Check-Tech programs (15 states authorized): average error detection rate equivalent to pharmacist check for routine fills; not authorized for high-alert medications in most states

Staffing Reality — Meridian Pharmacy Group

  • Average pharmacist coverage: 1.4 pharmacists per shift
  • Peak hours with only 1 pharmacist on duty: ~35% of operating hours
  • Current fill volume: approximately 280–340 prescriptions per shift at high-volume locations
  • Staff satisfaction survey: 68% of technicians cited 'time pressure' as top safety concern

agenda.label: Round 1
agenda.title: Understand the Risk
agenda.desc: What does the near-miss tell us and what problem are we actually solving?

agenda.label: Round 2
agenda.title: Debate the Options
agenda.desc: What are the real tradeoffs? Who bears the burden of each choice?

agenda.label: Round 3
agenda.title: Land the Recommendation
agenda.desc: What can the pharmacy director actually implement and defend?

message_schedule.text: I want to be direct about what the near-miss tells us: a 10-fold insulin dosing error was caught at the bedside, not at our verification step. That means our current single-pharmacist final check didn't catch it. Two-pharmacist independent double-check is the ISMP standard and it exists exactly for cases like this. The question isn't whether to add the check — it's how.

I hear Patel, but I need us to be honest about what 1.4 pharmacists per shift means in practice. On a busy Saturday afternoon with 300 fills, requiring a second pharmacist sign-off on every insulin prescription means that second pharmacist doesn't exist 35% of our operating hours. We're not creating a safety net — we're creating a policy that gets ignored under pressure, which is worse than not having the policy.

The data tells me the risk is concentrated. Insulin and anticoagulants are 58% of our errors and near-misses. If we scope this correctly — high-alert meds, not every prescription — the volume burden drops significantly. And the 4.2-minute average wait time increase from the ASHP study is for every prescription. Scoped to insulin and anticoagulants specifically, we're talking maybe 15–20 verification events per shift, not 300.

Lin's point on scoping is valid — I'd accept a limited implementation over a full implementation that gets abandoned at 2pm on a Friday. But I want to push back on the pilot option. We already know from 73% of hospital programs that double-checks reduce high-alert errors. We're not learning anything new from a 60-day pilot — we're just delaying a decision we already have the evidence to make.

Patel, the hospital data doesn't transfer directly to a retail pharmacy workflow — hospital pharmacists have different staff ratios and they're not also triaging walk-in patients and answering phones. That context matters. That said, I'd actually be most interested in the Tech-Check-Tech option — if we get TCT certified for routine fills, that frees pharmacist time for high-alert verification without adding overall burden. Has anyone looked at whether our state authorizes it?

Our state does authorize TCT for routine fills but not for high-alert medications — which is actually the right carve-out. So TCT plus limited double-check for the top-5 high-alert classes is operationally compatible. That's potentially the path: TCT frees pharmacist bandwidth, double-check absorbs it for high-alert meds. Does that math work, Marcus?

I could support that combination. It's not everything I'd want — full ISMP double-check for all high-alert meds — but it targets the actual risk concentration and it's actually implementable. If we're going to protect patients, a policy that's followed matters more than a perfect policy that's ignored.

That works for me if we're specific: TCT certification for routine fills, mandatory independent double-check for insulin/anticoagulants/concentrated electrolytes, and a 90-day review to assess wait time impact. I also want a clear protocol for when the second pharmacist genuinely isn't available — not 'find one somehow,' an actual documented exception process.

Agreed on the exception process — that's what turns this from a policy into a system. I'd summarize our recommendation as: TCT program for routine fills, two-pharmacist independent double-check for top-5 high-alert medication classes, documented exception protocol for single-coverage shifts, and 90-day QI review. What's the group's final position?

coaching_tips: Name the safety principle behind your position — 'independent double-check' means more than 'someone else checks it.'
Marcus raises a real operational constraint. Engaging it directly ('you're right that 35% of shifts have single coverage') is stronger than dismissing it.
The best collaborative contributions build on what was said: 'Lin's scoping point changes the burden analysis significantly...'
An exception protocol isn't a weakness in the policy — it's what makes it implementable. Advocate for clarity over perfection.
Move toward specifics in Round 3: which high-alert classes, what counts as training complete, who owns the 90-day review.
